generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee outside these ancient walls, and the sea, that restless thing, roiling and foaming as if it knows the hour is upon us. The lantern has been lit, as it must be, and the light swings with a steady, unyielding rhythm, as though it alone can hold the world together.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left me. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not even in the silence of the night, when the sea whispers secrets to the rocks and the stars seem to weep. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in a lifetime.

You were a bright thing, my Eleanor, like the light that guides the ships through the dark. I used to say that you were the light of my life, and perhaps I was not wrong. You were young, and you had dreams, and I, in my foolish pride, thought I could shield you from the world’s cruelty. But I was wrong, as I have been in so many things.

I do not write this to beg for forgiveness, for I know you have long since given that. Nor do I write to explain, for what man could explain the choices that bind a father’s heart? I only write because I fear I may not live to see another dawn. The sea is

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these ancient walls. The sea has been restless all week, and tonight, the storm is set to break with a fury that has not touched these shores in decades. I fear I may not see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of nights spent staring at the horizon, wondering if you ever thought of me at all. I have kept the lighthouse burning, as I always have, but the light is dimmer now. Not because the lantern is faulty, but because the fire in my heart has long since gone out.

You were young when you left, no older than seventeen, with dreams of the city and a heart full of ambition. I remember the day you came to me, your eyes alight with a fire I could not extinguish. "I must go, Father," you said, and I could not stop you. I told myself it was for the best. That you would find your place in the world, that you would be happy. But the world is not kind to those who leave behind the only home they've ever known.

I have not seen you since that day, and I have not written to you. I told myself it was because I could not bear the thought of you returning, of seeing the disappointment in your eyes. But now, as the storm gathers

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned outside this creaking tower. The sea roars, a beast unchained, and I feel the very bones of this old lighthouse groan beneath the weight of the storm. I pray you are safe, wherever you may be, and that the world has not yet claimed you in its relentless march.

Five years have passed since your last letter, and I have not dared to write to you. The silence between us has grown like the fog that clings to these cliffs at dawn—thick, suffocating, and impossible to pierce. I know you blame me for the way things ended, for the way I chose duty over the warmth of a home, over the promise of a family. And perhaps, in the cold light of truth, I must admit that I was wrong to let the sea take precedence over the life you deserved.

You were a child when I left, and I thought I was protecting you. I believed that the world was too cruel, too vast, and that the sea, in all its majesty and menace, would offer you a future more certain than the one I could provide. But I was a fool, Eleanor. A man who saw only the horizon, and not the heart that beat behind it.

I have not seen you in years, and I have not heard your voice. I have not heard the laughter

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles, not from the cold, but from the weight of the silence that has stretched between us like the sea between this island and the mainland. Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. Not a letter, not a single line. I have tried to forget, but the sea does not forget. It remembers every storm, every wave, every whisper of the wind.

Tonight, the sky is dark as a widow’s veil, and the wind howls like the ghost of a child. The sea is restless, and the lighthouse stands alone, a sentinel against the chaos. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, but I do not know if I will see the dawn. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can speak to you once more.

I have never been good with words, Eleanor. I have spent my life watching the sea, not speaking of my pain, not sharing my fears. But tonight, I must try. You were my light, once. The one thing that made me believe in something greater than the sea. You were my daughter, my heart, my hope. And now, I fear I have lost you.

I do not know why you left. I do not know if you still think of me, or if you have moved on. But I cannot stop thinking of you. I dream of

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside these ancient walls, and the sea roaring as though it seeks to reclaim what it once held dear. The lantern above me flickers with a feeble light, and I fear that when the storm is done, it may be the last time I shall ever see the sky.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and the silence between us has grown like the salt upon these rocks—hard, unyielding, and heavy. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried to reach out, to call you by name, to remind you of the child who once ran through the fields with me, who once laughed in the dawn as the sea kissed the shore. But the world has moved on, and I have remained here, a solitary sentinel, watching over the waves, waiting for the day when the tide might turn.

You left without a word, like the tide that retreats at dusk, leaving behind only echoes of what was. I did not blame you, not truly. I understood the weight of the world upon your shoulders, the burdens you bore, the choices you made. But I also felt the ache of loss, the loneliness of a father who could not hold his daughter close.

Tonight, however, is different. The storm is coming—not just in the sky, but in my heart. The sea has been restless all week, and the wind has

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**

*To My Dearest Eliza,*

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast unleashed, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs that hold this lighthouse in place. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

Five years have passed since your last letter, and the silence has grown heavier than the stones of this tower. I have not spoken to you in so long, and yet I have never stopped thinking of you. You were but a child when you left, and now you are a woman with a life of your own, a life I have not been part of. I do not know if I have the right to write to you, to beg for your forgiveness, or even to ask for your return. But I must try.

You were always the light in my life, Eliza. Even when you were small, you had a way of seeing through the gloom. I remember the nights you would come to the tower with your father, and we would watch the stars, or the moon, or sometimes—when the sky was clear—the distant ships that passed our way. You would ask questions, always so many, and I would answer them, even when I did not know the answers. You were curious, and that made you wise. I wish I had been a better father.

I know you were angry. I know you thought

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of a man who has long since stopped believing in miracles, yet tonight, as the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars with a fury I’ve never known, I find myself reaching for hope—perhaps even for you.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left this lighthouse, and me, behind. I’ve counted every day, every hour, every minute since the day you stormed out, your voice sharp as the salt wind, your eyes colder than the North Sea in winter. You said I was a fool, a drunkard, a man who had forgotten how to live. I suppose you were right. I have forgotten how to live. I’ve forgotten how to love.

But I have not forgotten you.

The storm is coming, my child. The sky is black as the void between stars, and the waves are rising like the wrath of the gods. I’ve spent my life watching the sea, reading its moods, its warnings. Tonight, it is warning me. The lighthouse has been lit for hours, its beam slicing through the darkness, but I fear it may not be enough. I fear that even if I survive this night, I may not live to see the dawn.

And if I don’t, I have one last thing to say to you.

You were my light, Eleanor. Not the lighthouse, not the beacon, but you. You were

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the very devil outside these walls. The sea roars, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and green. I’ve been watching the waves for hours, their fury growing with every passing moment. I fear I may not see dawn, and if I do, I fear I may not see you again.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left. I’ve tried to forget, but the sea doesn’t forget. It carries the weight of every storm, every sorrow, every secret. And I’ve carried mine, too.

You were young then, full of fire and defiance, and I was too stubborn to see the storm coming. I thought I could keep you safe, keep you from the world that had already taken too much from you. But you were not meant to be caged, not even by a father’s love. You had your own path, and I was too blind to see it.

I’ve thought of you every night, Eliza. Not just in the quiet hours when the lighthouse is still and the world is silent, but in the chaos of the storm, when the wind screams and the sea breaks against the rocks like a thousand voices crying out. I’ve thought of your laughter, your dreams, the way you used to sit by the window and sketch the stars. I’ve thought of the day you left,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I am. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have not written to you, nor have I seen your face, since the day you left. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried to forget, but the sea has a way of bringing memories to the surface, and the wind, it carries them with it.

You were a fine girl, Eleanor. You had a mind like a blade, and a heart that could have cut through the thickest of storms. I know you must have thought me a fool to let you go, but I had no choice. I could not bear to see you with a man who did not love you as I did. You were my light, my beacon in the darkness. And I let you go, thinking that it was for the best.

But now, as I sit here in the lighthouse, the wind howling like a banshee, I wonder if I was wrong. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I will survive it. I have always been a man of faith, but tonight, I find myself questioning everything. The sea is restless, the sky is black as ink, and I feel the weight of the world upon my shoulders.

I have not told anyone about the storm, not even the other keepers. I would not burden them with my fears. But I have been thinking of you,

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that has long since grown cold—yet tonight, as the wind howls like a beast from the depths of the sea, and the sky darkens as if the heavens themselves weep, I find myself compelled to reach out to you.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I last heard your voice. The days have worn on like the tides, relentless and unrelenting. I’ve kept the lighthouse burning, as I always have, for you, for the sea, for the memory of the child who once ran through these halls with laughter that could rival the crashing waves.

I know you think me a fool, a man who clings to the past like a sailor to a rope in a storm. You were right to think so. I was blind, and I was stubborn. I let my pride keep me from seeing the truth—how much I missed you, how much I needed you, how much I still do. I was too afraid to reach out, too afraid that if I did, you would turn away again.

But now, as the storm approaches, and I can feel the very walls of this lighthouse trembling with the weight of the wind, I find myself wondering if I will see another dawn. The sea has always been a cruel mistress, and tonight, she is more so than ever

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since I last saw you, and I fear the distance between us grows not only in miles, but in the very marrow of my soul. I write this with a trembling hand, for the storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

You were once the light of my life, and now, with each passing day, it seems the darkness of our separation has grown ever more suffocating. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried with all the strength that God and man have given me, to reach out to you, to mend what was broken. But you have turned your back, and I have not the courage to call you back. Not yet.

I have spent these years watching the sea, the ever-changing face of the horizon, the relentless tide that comes in, again and again, as if it knows no end. And so it is with the sea, I suppose, that I have come to understand my own heart. It is restless, it is longing, and it is broken. I have not spoken to you in years, and I fear I may never again. But I cannot let this silence be the end of our story.

The storm is coming, and it is fierce. The winds howl like the voices of the dead, and the waves rise like mountains. I have seen many storms in my time, but this one feels different. It is as if

**April 12, 1893**  
**St. Brigid's Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has long since grown cold, yet still beats in the rhythm of a father’s love—though I fear it is not love that I now feel for you, but only the weight of years unspoken and wounds unhealed.  

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since I last saw your face, heard your voice, or held your hand. The sea has been cruel to me, as it has to all who dwell upon its edge. It has taken the wind, the waves, and the light from my life. But tonight, as the storm gathers in the dark, I find myself haunted not by the sea, but by the silence of your absence.  

You were but a child when you left, Eleanor. A child who, in her heart, believed herself to be the victim of a cruel and unjust world. I was a fool to let her go. A fool to believe that a letter, a promise, or even a prayer could mend the rift between us. You left me with the words, *“You don’t understand,”* and I let you go. But I have spent these years trying to understand, and I have found only regret.  

I have watched the light burn through the night, through the gales, through the

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lonely tower. The sea roars, as if it too knows the weight of the night, and I find myself wondering—*will I survive this?* I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the storm gathers, I feel the need to say something, anything, to you.

You were a bright light once, Eleanor—brighter than this lighthouse, brighter than the stars that guide the ships through the dark. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes burned with something I could not understand. You called me a "prison warden," and I let you go, thinking it for the best. But I was wrong. I was always wrong. I let you go, and I lost you.

I have kept this tower for years, not for the glory of it, but because it was all I had left. The sea, the wind, the endless dark—these are the only things that make sense to me. But now, as the storm approaches, I find myself thinking of you. I think of the way you used to sit by the window, watching the waves, as if you were waiting for something. I think of your laughter, your kindness, the way you used to help me with the lamp, even when I told you not to. You were always so full of

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and silver. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, though I fear it may be the last time I can. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I shall see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have not softened the ache of your absence. I have not spoken to you in that time, nor have I written. But tonight, as the gales grow louder and the waves crash with a fury that seems almost human, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, to the past, to the silence that has grown between us.

You were a child when you ran away, as all children do, I suppose. But you did not return. I waited, as any father would, hoping that one day you would come back with explanations, with apologies, with the promise that you would not abandon me again. But you did not. And I did not. We both carried our wounds like ghosts, unable to speak the words that might have healed us.

I have always believed that you were better off without me. That the world was too cruel, too cold, and that the sea—this merciless, beautiful sea—was not a place for a girl like you. I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the narrow window of this old lighthouse, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars with a fury that would make even the bravest sailor quiver. Tonight is no ordinary night, Eliza. It is the night of reckoning, the night when the heavens themselves seem to conspire against us. And yet, as I sit here by the flickering light of the oil lamp, I find myself thinking not of the storm, but of you.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have done little to soften the ache of your absence. I have watched the horizon for you, Eliza, and the sea has never returned you. I have watched the stars, and the moon, and the sun, but none of them have brought you back to me. I have written to you, though I fear my words were too heavy with sorrow, and perhaps too much of a reminder of the life I could not give you. I was a lighthouse keeper, not a father. I was a man bound to the sea, not to the heart.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of my years pressing upon me, and I think of the choices we made. I was not cruel, Eliza, nor was I unkind. I believed that the world was too vast, too

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy as the storm that brews on the horizon that I write to you. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. I have stood at my post for over thirty years, and never before have I felt so alone. Tonight, I fear, may be my last.

You were but a child when you left this place, and I did not stop you. I told myself it was for the best, that you needed the world beyond these stones and the salt air. But now, as I sit in the glow of the oil lamp, I find myself longing for your voice, your laughter, the way you used to run through the dunes with the dogs and call me "Papa." I miss you more than I can say.

Five years have passed since you walked out of this house, and I have not heard from you since. I have tried to write, but the letters always returned unopened. I do not know if you still remember me, or if you have forgotten the man who once held you in his arms and sang you to sleep. I only know that the silence between us is as deep and unyielding as the sea.

I have always believed in duty, Eleanor. I have spent my life watching over this lighthouse, ensuring that no ship is lost to the dark. But now, as I prepare for the storm, I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with the weight of years. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and I have not spoken to you in that time. The silence between us has grown like the sea, vast, unyielding, and ever deep. I have tried to write to you many times, but the words would not come, and I feared that even if they did, you would not want to hear them.

Tonight, the wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars with a fury that I have never known. The lighthouse stands tall, but I fear that even it may not withstand the storm that is coming. I have spent my life keeping the light burning, guiding ships through the darkest nights, and now, I fear that I may not be able to keep the light alive for much longer.

I have thought of you often, Eliza. I have thought of the days when you were a child, running up the steps of the lighthouse, your laughter echoing through the stone walls. You used to call me "Papa" with such warmth, and I would tell you stories of the sea, of the stars, and of the great mysteries that lie beyond the horizon. But those days are gone, and I fear that I have lost more than just your company.

I do not know why we fell apart, child. I do not know if it was my

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls. The sea, that restless thing, roars with a fury I’ve never known. The storm is coming, and I fear it may claim me before the dawn. Yet, in this moment, I find myself compelled to write, not only to the sea, but to you—my daughter, my only child, whose name I have not spoken in five long years.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left. Five years since you slammed the door behind you, leaving me with nothing but the salt air, the creak of the beacon, and the ghosts of your laughter. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face, nor felt the warmth of your presence. The world has moved on, and I have remained here, a solitary sentinel against the endless dark.

I know you blame me. I know you believe I chose the lighthouse over you, that I abandoned you to the life you desired. But I ask you now, as the heavens weep and the waves rise like the wrath of God, to remember: I chose this life not for comfort, but for duty. The sea is a cruel mistress, and I have spent my days ensuring that no soul is lost to its fickle moods. I have stood watch when the world slept, and I have cried when the light failed. **Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

*To My Dearest Eleanor,*

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old tower. The sea roars with a fury I’ve never known, and I fear the storm may take more than the light. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

You were a child when you left, not with a tear or a goodbye, but with a silence so deep it seemed to swallow the very air. I remember the day you walked out of our house, your small frame wrapped in a shawl too large for you, your eyes full of a fire I could not understand. You said you were going to find your own way, and I did not stop you. I did not even say a word. I thought, *Perhaps it is for the best*. But I was wrong.

You were not the only one who left that day. I left too, in my own way. I left the warmth of your mother’s embrace, the laughter of your brothers, the quiet comfort of a home that had grown too small for my heart. I chose this lighthouse, this lonely existence, and I have stayed here, tending to the light, while the world moved on without me.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I wonder if I have made the right choice. I have spent years watching

**My Dearest Elara,**

It is with a heart heavy with regret and a mind clouded by the weight of years unspoken that I take up this pen. The storm is upon us, as fierce and unrelenting as the tempests that have long been a companion to my life on this lonely coast. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, ever restless, roars with a fury that makes me wonder if I shall see the dawn.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though time has worn the edges of our quarrel, it has not softened the sting of it. I have tried, Elara, I have tried to forgive, to forget, to move on. But the sea does not forget, and neither do I. You were my light in the darkest hours, and now, in this final hour, I find myself reaching for that light once more.

I have spent these years in the solitude of this lighthouse, tending to its needs, watching over the waves, and listening to the silence that has grown between us. I have thought of you often, more than I care to admit. I have seen your face in the mist, heard your laughter in the wind, and felt your absence in every breath of the cold sea air. You were my daughter, my heart, and I have failed you in so many ways.

I do not know if I will survive this storm, Elara. The sea has

**To My Dearest Elara,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee just beyond the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear it may not be long before it takes what little remains of me. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have no one else to say these things to.

You were a child when you left, no older than twelve, and I do not know if you remember it. I remember the day you ran from the house, your small frame disappearing into the mist, your voice echoing with a sorrow I could not understand. You said you wanted to be free, to see the world, to live your own life. I told you I would always be here, that I would always be your father. But you did not believe me.

I have tried, Elara. I have tried to be the man you needed, to be the father you deserved. I’ve kept the lighthouse lit, day and night, for years. I’ve tended the lamp, swept the floors, and listened to the waves. I’ve even taken to writing in this journal, though I have never sent it anywhere. You would not have read it, I suppose. You would have thought it just another old man’s foolishness.

But tonight is different. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I will make it through. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it may find you in a state of bewilderment. For I have not written to you in five years, and yet here I am, quill in hand, heart heavy, and soul trembling with the weight of words I have long kept locked away.

It has been a long time since we last spoke, and I confess, I have often wondered if you would ever write to me again. You were always the clever one, Eleanor, the one who could find a way out of any situation, even one as tangled as our own. But I fear I have been the one to tie the knots, and I have not been the one to untangle them.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man who thought I could keep you safe. I thought the sea was the only danger, and that if I stood watch, I could shield you from the world. But I was wrong. The world is vast, and it does not care for the wishes of a lighthouse keeper. And I, in my stubbornness, let my pride keep me from seeing the truth—*you were not meant to stay.*

I have not spoken to you because I did not know how. I did not know how to say that I loved you, and that I missed you, and that I was sorry. I thought that by keeping my distance, I was protecting you. But

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart full of sorrow, for the night is dark, the wind howls like the devil himself, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, but I must write, for if I do not, I fear I shall never speak to you again.

Five years have passed since you left this house, since you turned your back on me, your mother, and the life we once shared. I have not heard a word from you, not a single letter, not even a single word of comfort. You were once my light, my greatest joy, and now I fear I have become a shadow in your eyes. I do not blame you, for I have been a poor father, a man who failed to understand you, who failed to listen, who failed to be there when you needed me most.

I remember the day you left. It was a quiet morning, the sun rising over the sea, and I thought, *this will pass*. I thought, *she will come back*. But you did not. You did not return for your mother’s funeral, nor for the funeral of the house, nor for the funeral of the man I once was. And I have not forgiven myself for that.

Tonight, the storm is coming. I have seen the sky turn black, the waves rise like mountains, and the wind

**April 12th, 1887**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has long since turned to stone, yet still beats for you. It has been five years since you left, and five years since I have spoken your name aloud. But tonight—tonight, the wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars with a fury I have never known. I fear I may not see dawn.

You were always a child of the sea, my dear. You loved its songs, its storms, its endless, shifting moods. I tried to teach you the stillness of the lighthouse, the discipline of the light, the quiet dignity of duty. But you were never meant to be bound by such things. You were born to the waves, and I, a man of the rocks, could not understand that.

I remember the day you left. It was a clear, spring morning, the kind that makes you believe the world will never change. You stood at the door, your coat too large, your eyes too bright, and said, *“I’m not like you, Father.”* I did not answer. I could not. You had already left me.

I have not seen the sea in five years, Eliza. I have not seen your face. I have not spoken your name. But I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the way

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the stone walls of this old lighthouse, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth beneath us. The sky is a swirling maelstrom of black and silver, and the waves crash against the cliff with a fury that makes my bones ache. I fear I may not see dawn. But if I do, I must speak—before the storm silences me forever.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have done little to mend the rift between us. I have not seen your face, nor heard your voice, since the day you stormed out with that foolish notion of a life beyond this rock and the sea. You said I was a man of stone, unmoved by your grief, and that I had no place in your world. You were wrong, Eleanor. I was not unmoved—I was broken, and I did not know how to rebuild.

I have spent these years watching the sea, not with the hope of finding something in its depths, but with the hope that one day, you would return. I have kept the light burning, not for the ships that pass, but for the chance that one of them might carry you back. I have told myself that perhaps, in time, you would come to see that this place—this lighthouse, this life—is not as barren as you believed. That I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. The sea has been restless these past few days, and the wind howls like a wounded beast through the stone walls of this lighthouse. I’ve spent the evening by the window, watching the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury I’ve never seen before. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than the sea can give.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet I still hear your laughter echoing in the hollows of this place. I’ve tried to forget, but the memories are as stubborn as the tides. **My Dearest Eliza,**

I did not know if it was grief, or duty, or something far worse.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not brought a single word from you. I’ve tried to write, to send letters, to call out to you from the cliffs, but you have never answered. I’ve been told by the village priest that your father has passed, and that you have gone to live with some distant cousin in the north. But I do not believe that. You were always more like me than anyone else. You had the same stubbornness, the same quiet strength, the same love for the sea that I have always held dear. And yet, you left us.

I have not spoken of you to the others. I keep the door locked when the villagers come to check on the lighthouse, and I make sure that no one sees the letters I write, or the photographs of you that I keep tucked away in a drawer beneath my bed.

**April 12th, 1897**  
**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee outside these ancient stones. The sea has been restless all day, and I fear the worst. The lighthouse stands as it always has, a sentinel against the dark, but tonight—tonight may be different.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left. I have not heard a word from you, not a single letter, not a single word of comfort or condemnation. I have tried to forget, but the past lingers like a ghost in the fog. You were my light, my only light, and now you are gone.

I suppose I should start by saying I forgive you. I have spent many nights staring into the abyss of the sea, wondering if you ever thought of me at all. Did you think of the nights I kept the lamp burning long after the last ship had passed? Did you think of the cold nights when I would sit by the fire, whispering to the silence, hoping someone would answer?

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with dreams and a heart full of fire. I was too proud to see the cracks in your world. I was too afraid to ask what had driven you away. I should have been there for you. I should have known.

**April 12th, 1897**  
**Lighthouse, St. Kilda, Scotland**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the stone walls of this cursed tower. The sea roars, and the sky is black as a funeral shroud. I fear I may not see dawn. And yet, I must write—because if I do not, I fear I may never speak to you again.  

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not been enough to dull the ache of your absence. I have not seen your face in a mirror, nor heard your voice in the wind, and I have not spoken to you, not once. We have been like two ships passing in the night, each bound for a different shore, and neither willing to turn back.
